California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
March 12, 199O
David L. Zaltsman
Deputy county Counsel
Napa County
1195 Third Street, Room 3o1
Napa, CA 9 4 559-3 o01
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. A-9o-150
Dear Mr . Zal,tsman:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Local Agency Formation Comnissioner John J. Mikolajcik
regarding the commissioner's responsibil ities under the conflict-
ofjinter6st provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/
OUESTION
May Co:nmissioner Mikolajcik participate in Local Agency
Formation Commission decisions concerning the incorporation of the
American Ca-nygn region of Napa County where the commj,ssioner owns
a residence, undeveloped real property and a business in the
region.
CONCLUSION
Since the decision to incorporate the Amerj,can canyon area
would clearly result in either the inclusion of the commissioner's
property in the boundaries of the city of American canyon, or
exclusion of the coronissioner's property fron the boundaries of
the city, the commissioner rnay not participate in decisions
concerning the incorporation of Arnerican Canyon.
FACTS
The Napa Local Agency rornation Conrnission (hereafter
rrLAFcor' ) is currently considering a petition for the reorganiza-
tion of the American canyon region of Napa county. The petition
L/ covernment Code Sections 81OOO-91015. ALf statutory refer-
ences are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Con-
nission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 180O0, et ses. AlI references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
428 J street, Suite 8oo . P.o. Box 8o7 o sacramento cA 958o4.o8o? o (916)322-5660


File No. A-90-L50
Page 2
requests LAFCO approval for the incorporation of American Canyon
and the establishnent of one of the current independent districts
providing services to the community as a subsidiary district of
the city. 
If LAFCO approves the petition, the petition will be
submitted to the Napa County Board of Supervisors for their
consideration and ultinately the public of Napa County to be voted
on in an election.
Comruissioner uikotajcik is a member of LAFCO and a supervisor
for Napa county. Commissioner Mikolajcik owns a residence, a
business and three undeveloped parcels of land in the American
canyon area. The unincorporated area of Napa County is subject to
a growth control initiative, 
liteasure A, which severely restricts
the number of residential building pernits that nay be issued in
any given year. If American canyon is incorporated, j,t hlill no
Ionger be subject to the restrictions on residential building
pernits. 
American Canyon contains 80 parcels and 6 percent of the
total population of the county.
ANALYSIS
The Politica1 Reform Act, was enacted by the people of the
State of california by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose of the
conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act is to ensure that
public officials, 
whether eLected or appointed, perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free frorn bias caused by their own
financial interests or the financial interests of persons lrho have
supported then. (section 81001(b) . )
In fur:-therance of this goa1, section 87100 prohibits any
public official 
fron rnaking, participating in naking, or otherwise
usj.ng his or her official position to influence a governmentaL
decision in which the official 
has a financial interest. 
A
"public official" 
is defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700
(topy enclosed) as every natural Person who is a uenber, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.
This definition would include a nember of the I"APco.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a rnaterial financial effect,
distinguishable fron the effect on the public Aenerally, on the
official or a Dember of his or her iuunediate fanily or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(b) Any real property in vhich the public of-
ficial has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.


Fi■e No. A-90-■50
Page 3
***
(d) Any business entity in vhich the PubLic
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103 (a) , (b) and (d) .
Commissioner Mikolajcik owns a single fanily residence, a
business and three undeveloped parcels of land in the American
Canyon area of LAFCo's jurisdiction. 
Commissj,oner Mikolajcik's
interest in each of his properties and his business is greater
than $1,oOO. Thus, the connissioner's property and business are
potentially disqualifying financial interests as defined in Sec-
tion 87103 .
Ho$rever, section 87103 specifies that a public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision onl-y if it is reasonablv foresee-
able that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public Arenerally, on the
official's 
financiat interest. 
whether the financial consequences
of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the tirne a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each
particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable
if there is a substantiat likelihood that it will occur.
certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (fn re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198, copy enclosed.)
You stated that the incorporation of American canyon would
refove the area from restrictions on the residential development
of property imposed by llteasure A. while financlal effects on the
comnislioner's residential propertY and business are uncertain, a
change in the ability to develop currently undeveloped Parcels
would have a certain financial effect on the value of his
undevel,oped property. This is so even if he does not intend to
develop the property at this tiDe. 
(In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC
ops. 1, copy enclosed.) Thus, we t oul,d conclude that such a deci-
sion pernj.tting the incorporation of the American Canyon area
would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the
financial interests of commissioner I'Iiko1ajcik.
In addition to a foreseeable financial effect, the effect on
the commissioner's real property must also be naterial. 
Regula-
tion 18702.1(a) (3) (A) (copy enclosed) provides that the effect of
a decision on real property in which an offj.cial has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest is rnaterial if:
The decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or
lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from anv citv,
countv, district or other local qovernmental


File No. A-90-150
Page 4
subdivision, of real property in which the official
has a direct or indirect interest (other than a
leasehol.d interest) of $1,000 or Dore, or a similar
decision affecting such property. 
(Enphasis
added. )
Since the decisj,on to incorporate the Amerj,can canyon area
would clearly result in the j.nclusion of the commissioner's
property in the planned City of American canyon, the effect on the
Lomnissioner's property is deemed to be naterial unless there urill
be no financial effect on the official's 
real property. 
(Regula-
tiot 18702.1(c) (2).) 
consequently, if there will be any financial
effect on Conmissioner Mikolajcik's real property, the commis-
sioner may not participate in decisions concerning the incorPora-
tion of American Canyon. (Regulation lA7O2.1(a) (3) (A).)
You have also asked if the coromissioner rnay participate in
the decisions concerning the j.ncorporation of American canyon if
the boundaries are reduced by I"AFco to exclude some or all of the
cornmissioner's property. The basic rule is that any single
financial interest in a decision is sufficient to require
disqualification. 
Thus, $rhere some, but not all of the
conmissioner's real property, is removed from the proposed
boundaries, the commj,ssioner sti1l tnay not participate-
}loreover, even if alt of the cornmissioner's undeveloped
property and his residence are excluded fron the boundaries of the
city, fre rnay stiII be required to disqualify hinself front
parlicipating in the decisions concerning American canyon where
Lhe decisien uitl have a roaterial financial effect on his
business. The effect of the decision on his business is nateri-al
if it will result in an increase or decrease in the gross revenues
or value of assets or liabilities 
of his business for a fiscal
year of $1O,Ooo or more; or, will result in his business incurring
-or avoiding additional exPenses or reducing or elininating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of 12,500 or
more.2/ (Reqnrlation 18702.2 (g), copy enclosed.)
Finally, where the boundaries of the proposed city have been
approved, without the commissioner's participation, to exclude a1I
oi-tis financial interests, the cornmissioner may participate in
the deliberations regarding other asPects of the proposal,
provided those deliberations do not result in a reopening or in
lny way affect the decisions from vrhich he was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343, copy enclosed.)
2l This is assuming the conrnissioner's
small one. Regulation L8702.2 provides
nateriality depending on the financial
concerned in the decision.
business is a relatively
differing standards of
size of the business entity


File No. A-90-150
Page 5
Interveninq Review
You have also asked whether the commissioner may participate
in the incorporation decision due to the intervening revi,ew of the
Napa county Board of supervisors and the voters of Napa County.
The prohibition on participating in decisions in which an official
has a conflict of interest includes both the uaking and the
influencing of governmental decision. (Section 8710o.) Thus,
despite the fact that the decisions of LAFco rnust be reviewed and
approved by the Napa county Board of Supervisors and then submit-
ted to the voters for approval, LAFCO Commissioners are sti1l
influencing the final decision on the matter.
This is because, an incorporation proposaL cannot be
presented to the county or the voters without an official act of
LAFCo even though the final adoption nust take place through an
el,ection. The menbers of LAFco are, therefore, participating in a
governmental decision whether or not to subroit the proposal to the
-ounty board of supervisors. A public official with a financial
interest in the outcone of the ultirnate decision urust disqualify
hinseLf from participation in any and all discussions, debate or
vote regarding that decision. (Skousen Advice Letter, No.
A-88-162 and Beniarnin Advice Letter, No. A-86-061, copies
enclosed. )31 
-
Public Generallv
Public officials with financial interests that will be
naterially affected by a decision Day participate in the decision
if the effe-ct-on their property is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. For the ttpublic generalfyrr excep-
tion to apply, a decision nust affect the official's 
interests in
substantially the sarne Danner as it would affect a significant
segment of the public. (Regarlation 18703, copy enclosed.)
The trpublicrr consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in guestion. (In re o$ren (L976t 2 FPPC ops. 77, coPy
enclosed.) This is so because all the residents of the jurisdic-
tion are constituents of the official. 
(In re Lecran, supra.) In
your tetter you asked whether the entire county would be the
jurisdiction of LAFco, or just the unincorporated portions of the
county. In In re Leoan, supra the CoDmission stated:
3/ If Connissioner Mikolajcik is the sole owner of the real
property and business which fono the basis for his disqualifica-
tion, or if he owns these interests jointly erith only his spouse
and dependent children he would be able to testify before the
LAFCo, in his personal capacity, in the sane manner as other
menbers of the public. His testinony would be lirnited to
representing his personal interests (i.e., he could not represent
other property or business owners before the LAFCO). (Reg'ulations
18700(d) (2) and 18700.1(b), copy enclosed.)


Fl■e No. A-90-■50
Page 6
[I]n the case of the Santa Clara County Board
of supervisors, the I'publicrr rrould consist of the
entire county of santa clara, even though its
priuary land use jurisdiction is confined to the
unincorporated areas of the county. The rrpublicrl
includes the entire jurisdiction for the agency in
guestion, particularly lrhere an elective body is
being considered, because aI1 of the County's
residents are constituents of the various members
of the Board of Supervisors and nany reside in the
various incorporated cities within the county.
They are all inpacted in one way or another by the
county's land-use decisions, even if not directly
subject to the county's land-use jurisdiction. 
we
see no reason to alter our interpretation that the
residents, and businesses and property owners of
the county of santa clara are the |tpublictr for
purposes of decisions being nade by the Board of
Supervisors .
while LAFco members are appointed public officj.als, the deci-
sions of LAFco nay and often do affect the incorporated cities of
the county, in rnuch the same vay as decisions of the county of
board of supervisors. consequently, lre conclude that for the
public generally exception to apply under these circunstances, a
decision Dust affect the couutissioner's interests in substantially
the same Danner as it would affect a significant segment of the
residents and persons doing business in Napa county.
The rule-of law, as enunciated in fn re Ferraro ' (L978) 4
FPPC ops. 62, ls that [in order to be considered a significant
segTnent of the pubic ... a group usually nust be Iarge in numbers
and heterogeneous in qualify.tr 
You stated in your letter that
only 6 percent of the total population of the county reside in the
American canyon area. Moreover, of those 6 percent it is unlikely
that all wilt be affected uniformly. The number of persons owning
undeveloped land in the area is fewer than 6 percent, thereby
further reducing the number of persons that can be considered rraf-
fected similarlytt to Conroissioner Mikolajcik. Thus, the segTnent
of Napa county's population that will be similarly affected by the
decj,sj,on is too smal,I to meet the requirements of the public
generally exception.
You also asked whether the rationale applied in the fn re
ovren, supra, would lead to a different result. 
In ohren the plan-
ning comrnission and city council of Davis were naking decisions
concerning land use in the "core areat' of the city. 
The decisions
included the expansion of conmerciaL facilities, 
designation of
one-way streets and increasing parking areas. The Conmission
concluded that residential honeowners in and immediately adjacent
to the core area of city srere a significant segnent of total
population of the City of Davis and hrould be sinilarly affected by
the decisions.


FiIe No. A-9o-150
Page 7
However, the t'pub1ic" in 9!gC!, aqrainst which the segment
sinilarly affected was coDpared, uas the snall population of a
relatively conrpact city. 
lloreover, the decisions rrere such that
no persons or groups in the core area would be uniqueLy affected.
Under the facts presented in your letter, the decision concerns a
srnall portion of the jurisdiction, containing only 6 percent of
the county,s population. Further, the decision is such that the
effect !ri11 be unigue on persons owning undeveloped land, and thus
hrould further reduce the nurnber of persons ttsimilarly affected.tl
Consequently, we believe that In re Owen is factually distinct and
is not decisive in analyzing the question in this letter. 
We
conclude that the 'ipublic generallytt exception does not apply to
these facts.
If any further questions regarding this natter, please feel
free to contact ne at (916) 322-59oL.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E, Donovan
counse I
ohn w.
ounsel,
wa 1lace
Legal Division
